course_description:
VETS3017 explores the major classes of drugs, chemicals, and physical agents used in veterinary therapeutics, outlining their mechanisms of action, clinical uses, side effects, contraindications, and dosage guidelines. The course also introduces key terminology and legislation relevant to veterinary pharmacology. Content is tailored to the needs of veterinary technologists working in clinical practice, research settings, and related fields.

In addition, VETS3017 covers foundational principles of toxicology, including how toxins affect the body, common toxicants encountered in small animal practice, and the recognition of associated clinical signs.

In response to student evaluation feedback regarding content load and assessment pressures, we will be implementing several changes to the course in 2026 to enhance learning, engagement, and overall manageability. These improvements include:

Please note that unexpected changes to the Timetable may occur throughout the Semester. Therefore, it is crucial to regularly check the course Blackboard site. Any updates to the timetable will be communicated to students via Blackboard Announcements.
course_aims: This course aims to prepare students for a veterinary support role in a clinical context, research facility or industry role (e.g. veterinary pharmaceutical company) with a focus on developing an understanding of the drugs and chemical agents used in veterinary therapeutics and their applications.

learning_outcomes.description: Classify drugs and chemical agents used in the therapy of companion animals and livestock, and explain their pharmacokinetics, physiological and pharmacological effects in different body systems.

Calculate correct doses, identify suitable routes of administration, and dispense drugs and chemicals in accordance with veterinary instruction and accompanied by appropriate directions to clients for a range of clinical scenarios.
Accurately record veterinary patient medical information relating to drug administration and therapeutic effects using appropriate terminology.
Demonstrate an understanding of and comply with safety legislation relevant to the use of veterinary drugs and chemical agents.
Know the basic principles of toxicology and how toxins affect organ systems with particular reference to the clinical signs, emergency treatment and management of specific toxicants in small animals.

Students will be able to work effectively in small groups to plan, develop, and deliver veterinary practice relevant tasks, demonstrating professional communication, shared responsibility, and collaborative problem-solving appropriate to veterinary technology roles.

assessment_details.title: Team Based Learning: A Case-Based Approach to Therapeutics for the VT (Anaesthesia and Analgesia)
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment is delivered as a Team Based Learning (TBL) session . Students will be assessed on their understanding and ability to apply knowledge of the pharmacology, side effects, and contraindications of anaesthetic and analgesic drugs. Students will also be assessed on their ability to calculate drug dosages in the forms commonly used, and on their understanding of relevant Queensland and National legislation governing their use. The assessment will take the form of TWO (2) multiple‑choice quizzes: Individual quiz: completed by each student independently. (10%) Group quiz: completed collaboratively in randomly allocated groups. (10%) This session will be conducted in‑person on campus, unless otherwise specified. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Students MUST be available for their examinations on the scheduled date, time, and campus of their course enrolment. The exam format is determined by the Course Coordinator and may include written (paper-based, online, or digital), practical, oral, or other timed assessments (such as quizzes).
If you are unable to sit for your original examination, you may be eligible for a deferred examination. To apply, you must demonstrate that unavoidable circumstances prevented you from attending your exam. If this is not possible, you can apply for a one-off discretionary deferred exam. All requests for deferred examinations MUST include supporting documentation. For more information, including acceptable evidence and application instructions, please visit my.UQ Deferring an Exam .
Please note that there are no provisions to defer an already-deferred exam. You must be available to sit your deferred examination at the allocated time.
Students who submit five or more deferral requests within a twelve-month period will be contacted with advice on the services and support available within the University.
NOTE: If an application for deferred assessment is approved, the students grade for the task will consist entirely of their individual quiz mark.

assessment_details.title: Assessment Sequence: Team Based Learning (Antimicrobials, Parasitology, Toxicology)
assessment_details.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Quiz, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Students are required to participate in 3 additional Team Based Learning (TBL) assessments. Session 1 Toxicology (5%) - Friday February 6. Team based learning session where students will be assessed on their understanding of the basic principles of toxicology, especially how toxins affect the body, and provide information on common toxicants that affect small animals and how to recognise clinical signs. Session 2 Antimicrobials (5%) - Friday February 20. This assessment is delivered as a team‑based learning session focused on the responsible use of antibiotics. Students will be assessed on their understanding of antibiotic pharmacology, pharmacokinetics, legislation governing antimicrobial use, and principles of antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Session 3 Parasiticides (5%) - Friday March 13. Team based learning session where students will be assessed on their understanding and ability to apply their knowledge of the pharmacology, side effects and contraindications of parasiticides and their understanding of the Queensland and National legislation governing their use. These sessions includes TWO (2) multiple‑choice quizzes: An individual quiz completed independently. (2.5% each quiz) Group quizzes completed collaboratively in randomly allocated teams. (2.5% each quiz) These sessions will be conducted in‑person on campus, unless otherwise specified. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assessment using the Inspera platform
Students must click the ‘SUBMIT NOW’ button on the summary page to submit their answers. After submission, a confirmation will appear on the dashboard indicating that the answers have been submitted and can no longer be edited.
For all exams lasting 60, 90, 120, or 180 minutes, an additional 10 minutes of planning time is provided to read the questions and plan responses, though students may start working immediately if they choose.
It’s crucial to ensure your laptop meets the device requirements for Inspera assessments before the exam. If you don’t have a suitable laptop, you can borrow one from the Library. Check Get Familiar with Inspera for the most up-to-date information about using the platform. Note that power outlets will not be available during exams.

assessment_details.title: RECOVER CPR training certification
assessment_details.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Performance, Practical/ Demonstration, Quiz, Reflection, Role play/ Simulation
assessment_details.weight: 15% (3% BLS online, 6% ALS online, 6% BLS/ALS in person training)
assessment_details.due_date: Practical component of RECOVER assessment to be held in the 3rd year exam block (week beginning April 13, 2025). Each student will be required to attend one full day for the "in person" RECOVER Rescuer certification during the assessment week.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Knowledge of pharmacology is critically important in CPR (cardiopulmonary resuscitation) training because the effective use of drugs can directly influence the outcome of resuscitation efforts. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) Basic Life Support (BLS) - online certification . The BLS module will be completed online during the nine week teaching block. It may be completed at any time once each student has been enrolled for the online component. - (3%). CPR Advanced Life Support (ALS) - online certification. The ALS module will be completed online during the nine week teaching block. It may be completed at any time once each student has been enrolled for the online component. - (6%). RECOVER Rescuer Certification - this will be a scheduled practical with instructor-led clinical simulations held during the exam block. The online BLS and ALS modules must be completed prior RECOVER Rescuer Certification. Students are expected to complete the online modules as Self Directed Learning (SDL) during the nine week teaching block. - (6%). Students will complete the online Basic Life Support and Advanced Life Support modules prior to completing instructor led clinical scenarios to gain RECOVER certification. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) - CPR BLS and CPR ALS Online Certification This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) - RECOVER Rescuer Certification This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
BLS and ALS online courses can be completed at any time throughout the semester and completion certificates must be uploaded to Bb - no later than Monday April 6.
Certificates for BLS and ALS completion must be uploaded through the course Blackboard page prior to the RECOVER Rescuer component of the assessment.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Students with acceptable reasons (medical or compassionate reasons or as a result of exceptional circumstances) may apply for an extension of the deadline. Information on applying for an extension can be found here: my.UQ Applying for an extension .
Extension applications must be received by the assessment due date and time.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time.
If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example: If you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).
10% will be deducted per day, for up to 7 calendar days, at which point your submission will receive a mark of zero (0) unless an extension has been approved

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: RECOVER Rescuer Certification The use of therapeutics in CPR is essential for informed, timely decision-making during resuscitation, and students will learn how to select and administer appropriate drugs within simulated clinical scenarios aligned with RECOVER guidelines. Students will complete the online Basic Life Support and Advanced Life Support modules prior to completing instructor led clinical scenarios to gain RECOVER certification.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 10 (19 Jan - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Team Based Learning Sessions (4 sessions) In 2026, the learning structure for Therapeutics will be enhanced through the introduction of team-based learning sessions, associated assessment tasks, and additional tutorials. These activities will build on lecture content and make use of authentic clinical application scenarios to strengthen students’ ability to apply theoretical knowledge in practice. The redesigned approach will support deeper engagement and skill development across key therapeutic domains, including anaesthesia and analgesia, antimicrobials, parasiticides, and toxicology, ensuring students build the confidence and competence required for contemporary veterinary technology practice.”

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 10 (19 Jan - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Lecture Series Series of lectures covering basic knowledge of pharmacology (pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics) and drugs classified by body system (e.g. cardiovascular, gastro-intestinal, respiratory, endocrine) OR by effects (e.g. antimicrobials; anti-inflammatories, chemotherapeutics) will be delivered over the semester. Modes of action, therapeutic effects, side effects, legislative classification, and other clinical applications will be discussed.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . You'll also need to be aware of the following policies and procedures while completing this course: Fitness to Practise Policy Learning activities
